State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

August 31, 1976

Ms. Dorothy Outwater, City Clerk
City of Alhambra
111 South First Street
Alhambra, California 91801

RE: Resolution of the City Council of the City of
Alhambra #R76-121-letter of Mrs. Yocum, Trustee
of the Alhambra Library Board.

Dear Ms. Outwater:

Thank you for your letter of July 30, 1976 and a copy of
Resolution R76-121 adopted by the Alhambra City Council opposing
the purported requirement of the Political Reform Act of 1974
that all members of appointive boards and commissions in the
City of Alhambra serving without pay be required to submit
statements of economic interest pursuant to a Conflict of Interest
Code. Please be advised that the Commission has undertaken steps
to insure that over-designation and over-disclosure not exist
within the conflict of interest code development and implementation
process on either the state or local level.

More specifically, at its meetings of August 4th and 5th, the
Commission issued statements of policy concerning the use of
the terms "foreseeably" and "materially" and revised its Regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(c), defining "participation
in the making of a governmental decision." These concepts are
vitally important to the development of conflict of interest codes
because it is only persons who occupy positions within an agency
which involve "the making or participation in the making of
decisions which may foreseeably have a material effect on any
financial interest," who will require to be designated officials
within a conflict of interest code. As is apparent, it is only a
designated official who will be required to file statements of
economic interest. Copies of the Commission's statements of
Policy and regulation changes, which have the effect of narrowing
scope of designations, are attached.


D. Outwater                    -2-                    August 31, 1976


     The Resolution of the Council speaks in general terms and
it is impossible to discern the nature of the disclosure require-
ments imposed upon members of boards and commissions such as that
of Mrs. Yocum, a Trustee of the Alhambra Library Board. However,
several points should be made with respect to the view expressed
by the City Council that the Political Reform Act of 1974 has
"resulted in excess investigation and problems of reporting so
as to become as threatening to the locating and disclosing of true
conflicts of interest...." First, it should be clear that the Act
contemplates a significant and genuine delegation of responsibility
to local agencies and local code reviewing bodies for the purpose
of determining who should be included within the coverage of
local conflicts of interest codes and those persons would be
compelled to disclose. Government Code Section 87301 provides:

     "It is the policy of this act that Conflict of Interest
     Codes shall be formulated at the most decentralized
     level possible, but without precluding intra-departmental
     review."

     In addition, Government Code Section 87302(b) provides:

          "An investment, interest real property or income shall
          be made reportable by the Conflict of Interest Code if
          the business entity in which the investment is held,
          the interest in real property, or the income or source
          of income may foreseeably be effected materially by any
          interest or decision made or participated in by the designated
          employee by virtue of his position."

     Thus, it is clear that a board or commission should take an active
role in determining what interests, if any, the Board is capable
of materially financially affecting. If the Trustees of the
Library Board and the Council determined that the Trustees do
make and participate in the making of decisions which foreseeably
could have a material financial affect on a financial interest,
disclosure categories and responsibilities should be so narrowly
drawn by the Board and approved by the City Council so as to
prevent unwarranted over-disclosure.

     I trust that this information will be of some assistance to
the Council and to board members such as Mrs. Yocum in carrying
out their responsibilities under the Political Reform Act.

     If I can be of further assistance to you please call me at
916/322-6444.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
                                        Chief, Conflicts of Interest
                                        Division

City of Alhambra
111 SOUTH FIRST STREET
ALHAMBRA, CALIFORNIA 91801
PHONE: 282-5111

July 30, 1976

OFFICE OF
DOROTHY OUTWATER, CMC
CITY CLERK

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Gentlemen:

Enclosed is a certified copy of Resolution R76-121 adopted
by the Alhambra City Council at their meeting of July 20,
1976, unanimously supporting the position stated by Mrs.
Norma Yocum, Member of the Alhambra Board of Library Trustees,
pertaining to financial disclosure under Proposition 9 for
Boards and Commissions in Alhambra, all members of which are
appointive and serve without pay, and directing the trans-
mittal of her statement to our State Legislators.

We trust this Resolution will be presented and considered
at the time of the Commission's meetings to be held on August 4th
and 5th on these matters.

Respectfully yours,

Dorothy Outwater
DOROTHY OUTWATER, CMC
CITY CLERK

DO:mw
Enclosure


RECEIVED
JUL 20 1976

Alhambra Public Library
410 WEST MAIN STREET • ALHAMBRA • CALIFORNIA 91801
(213) 289-4216

CITY CLERK
E. Caswell Parry

July 13, 1976

RE: Financial Disclosure for Boards and Commissions in Alhambra
    All of whom are appointive positions, serving without pay

This statement was read at the regular meeting of the Alhambra Library
Board on July 13, 1976, and was unanimously approved by the Board and
is hereby read into the record of the Alhambra City Council at its
regular meeting of July 20, 1976.

It is hard enough to get successful and capable people to enter into
public life; filling both elective and appointive positions. But, for
government to pry into the personal lives of volunteer citizens by
requiring disclosure of their financial holdings and investments in such
a manner as to make them public knowledge by putting them on public
display is to create an obstacle too great to tolerate. We recognize
that the quick response will be that the Council has no alternative,
since the people wrote this law by voting for Proposition 9. But we
believe that someone needs to draw to the attention of the people this
gross error so that the present law can and will be changed. To simply
submit to this invasion of private matters will not correct the
situation.

Filing the requested form will not prevent any wrong-doing by any public
official who is prone to do wrong. Suspected wrong-doing must still be
discovered, proved, and prosecuted for the public to be benefitted.

So far as employees are concerned, we feel that this disclosure law will
not prove to be beneficial either. The supervision of employees by
authorized management is sufficient to prevent actions which might be in
conflict with personal gain. And again, this is the procedure which
would have to be followed in the discovery, proof and prosecution of
wrong-doing. For the many to be forced to file financial holdings,
investments, and savings for the prying eyes of those who would request
to see them is an unwarranted invasion of their personal lives, far
out-weighing any possible benefit.

In light of the accepted custom of employees in private enterprise to
own stock and to participate in profit-sharing plans in the companies
they are employed by, employees are accustomed to perform their jobs in
such manner as to be a benefit to their employer. This we expect from
employees of public agencies as well, and the vast majority do perform in
this manner.

Exhibit "A"

1  with the thought of recommending the submission to the electorate
2  of this State either an Assembly or Senate Concurrent Amendment
3  which would correct the extreme and impracticable provisions
4  in Proposition 9 which have resulted in excess invasions of
5  privacy and problems of reporting so cumbersome as to thwart the
6  locating and disclosing of true conflicts of interest which
7  the Constitutional Amendment was intended to cover.  The toll of
8  the unreasonable features of the present Constitutional Amendment
9  has already resulted in an unconscionable loss of elected and
10 appointed officials in the City of Alhambra, in particular, and
11 in the State of California, generally.
12
         SECTION TWO:  The City Clerk is hereby directed to send
13 copies of this resolution to Governor Brown, our State Legislators,
14 the Fair Political Practices Commission and the League of
15 California Cities.
16
         Signed and approved this 20th day of July, 1976.
17

                                             Mayor

24 was duly passed and adopted by the Alhambra City Council at its
25 regular meeting held on the 20th day of July, 1976, by the
26 following vote, to wit:

28 NOES:    NONE
29 ABSENT:   COUNCILMAN DUNCAN

    full, true, and correct copy of the original on file in the office of the City Clerk of the
    City of Alhambra, California.    [illegible] No 07 76-12/
                                      Supplied July 22, 1976
                                      on file in the office of the City Clerk of the
                                      City of Alhambra, California.
                                      [illegible] Outwater
                                      City Clerk
32

RESOLUTION NO. R76-121

A RESOLUTION OF THE ALHAMBRA CITY COUNCIL
UNANIMOUSLY SUPPORTING THE POSITION STATED
BY MRS. NORMA YOCUM, MEMBER OF THE ALHAMBRA
BOARD OF LIBRARY TRUSTEES, PERTAINING TO
FINANCIAL DISCLOSURE UNDER PROPOSITION 9 FOR
BOARDS AND COMMISSIONS IN ALHAMBRA, ALL MEMBERS
OF WHICH ARE APPOINTIVE AND SERVE WITHOUT PAY,
AND DIRECTING THE TRANSMITTAL OF HER STATEMENT
TO OUR STATE LEGISLATORS

WHEREAS, the Board of Library Trustees of the City of
Alhambra unanimously approved the position stated by one of its
trustees, Mrs. Norma Yocum, pertaining to financial disclosure
for boards and commissions in Alhambra, all members of which are
appointive and serve without pay; and,

WHEREAS, a copy of Mrs. Yocum's statement is attached
hereto, marked Exhibit "A", and made a part hereof; and,

WHEREAS, such statement was read into the record of the
Alhambra City Council at its regular meeting held on July 20,
1976; and,

WHEREAS, the Alhambra City Council unanimously supports
the position set forth in such statement and directs that a copy
of her statement be sent to the State Legislature as this Council
firmly believes Mrs. Yocum's position is indicative of the
position held by the majority of such board and commission
members, not only in Alhambra, but also throughout the State of
California;

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED by the Alhambra City
Council as follows:

SECTION ONE: This Council hereby coincidentally
recommends to the Legislature of the State of California that it
institute action to cause modification of Proposition 9 in
accordance with the objectives as stated in Exhibit "A" hereof;
and hereby memorializes the Legislature to undertake studies in
appropriate Senate and Assembly Committees to study Proposition 9

But to assume that short of making public disclosure of personal affairs,
employees and officials WILL SEEK ULTERIOR MEANS TO BE DISHONEST is not
the American way. It is a cardinal rule in this country that a person
be assumed to be honest UNTIL PROVEN OTHERWISE. This FINANCIAL DISCLOSURE
LAW is contrary to this accepted belief. The American Revolution was
fought for the right of a citizen to privacy in his personal affairs.

I, for one, refuse to submit to this requirement to hold office as a
trustee of the Alhambra Public Library. I have already by many ways
proved to the library, the city government, and the people of Alhambra
that I am loyal, honest and honorable, and industrious in my efforts to
serve in public office.

The members of the Alhambra Public Library Board, having already taken
an oath of office in which they promised to serve in this capacity to the
best of their ability and judgement, submit that they shall voluntarily
remove themselves from the decision-making process in any and all cases
where there could be a possible conflict of interest. Therefore, it is
our decision that the members of the Library Board need not file under
the Conflict of Interest Code.

Signed

Norma K. Yocum

Norma Yocum, Trustee Member
Alhambra Library Board
